message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Little is known about the interplay between gut microbiome and SARS-CoV-2 vaccine immunogenicity. In this prospective observational study, we investigated associations between the gut microbiome, habitual dietary fibre intake, and mRNA vaccine-elicited immune responses, including anti-Spike IgG, avidity, and ACE-2 competition (surrogate neutralization).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    16S rRNA sequencing and short-chain fatty acid analyses were undertaken using stool samples collected from 48 healthy individuals at baseline and twelve-weeks after 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    BNT162b2 SARS-CoV-2 vaccine dose. Associations between gut microbiome data and SARS-CoV-2 spike and RBD IgG levels, competitive binding antibodies, and anti-SARS-CoV-2 spike total relative fractional avidity assays were evaluated. A validated dietary fibre intake food frequency questionnaire was also used to correlate habitual dietary fibre intakes with vaccine responses.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Our data revealed several baseline bacterial taxa, including
                    <jats:italic>Prevotella, Haemophilus</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>Veillonella</jats:italic>
                    (p<0.01), associated with BNT162b2 vaccine responses. Several
                    <jats:italic>Bacteroides</jats:italic>
                    spp. (p<0.01) as well as
                    <jats:italic>Bifidobacterium animalis</jats:italic>
                    , (p=0.003), amongst others, were positively associated with antibody avidity. Conversely, concentrations of isovaleric and isobutyric acid were higher in individuals with the lowest SARS-CoV-2 vaccine responses (p<0.01). Classifying participants based on habitual dietary fibre intake identified distinct avidity responses.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We showed associations between baseline gut microbiota composition and immunogenicity of BNT162b2 vaccine responses, particularly avidity maturation. We also demonstrate that branched-chain fatty acids and habitual dietary fibre intakes are associated with BNT162b2 vaccine immunogenicity. Together these findings indicate a link between gut microbiome, diet and antibody immunity to SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, suggesting interventions which modulate the gut microbiome could enhance COVID-19 vaccine responses.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>SIGNIFICANCE OF THIS STUDY</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is already known on this subject?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Strength and persistence of the SARS-CoV-2 BNT162b2 vaccine is variable between individuals.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>To date, only one study has demonstrated that baseline gut microbiota can predict SARS-CoV-2 vaccine response.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What are the new findings?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>For the first time we showed that the higher concentrations of branched-chain fatty acids, isovaleric and isobutyric acids, are negatively associated with SARS-CoV-2 BNT162b2 vaccine responses.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          We revealed that habitual dietary fibre intake led to variability in the strength of antibody binding after the BNT162b2 vaccine. Specifically, high dietary fibre consumers displayed a significant increase in antibody avidity between in their 1
                          <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                          and 2
                          <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                          dose.
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>How this study might affect research, practice, or policy</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Our data suggests that therapeutic interventions which target the gut microbiome, including dietary modification, as well as pre-, pro-, and post-biotics, could enhance BNT162b2 vaccine immunogenicity, thus helping in the fight against COVID-19.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Variability in mRNA SARS-CoV-2 BNT162b2 vaccine immunogenicity is associated with differences in the gut microbiome and habitual dietary fibre intake

reference.unstructured: Bartolo L , Afroz S , Pan Y-G , et al. SARS-CoV-2-specific T cells in unexposed adults display broad trafficking potential and cross-react with commensal antigens. Sci Immunol. 2022;3127. https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2021.11.29.470421v1 https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2021.11.29.470421v1.abstract.
